reasoning: The development of a vaccine for Paracoccidioidomycosis (PCM) is highly unlikely within the next 5-10 years. Currently, there are no approved human vaccines for any fungal infection. While research is ongoing (such as the P10 peptide vaccine), it is largely confined to preclinical murine models and is intended as a therapeutic adjuvant rather than a preventative vaccine. A broad-spectrum pan-fungal vaccine (from UGA) is just entering Phase 1 clinical trials as of mid-2025, but vaccine development typically takes 10-15 years minimum to move from Phase 1 to approval, particularly for neglected tropical diseases which chronically suffer from underfunding and logistical hurdles in conducting large efficacy trials. Therefore, the chance of a vaccine being developed, approved, and recorded on Wikipedia is effectively zero for the near-term dates (2026-2029). By 2031, there is a marginal chance (5%) if early trials are fast-tracked or mRNA technology accelerates timelines drastically. By 2036, there is a slightly higher chance (12%) that broader fungal vaccines entering trials today will have come to fruition and include coverage for PCM.
